disease.name: Hepatitis A
disease.note: Acute self-limited viral hepatitis caused by HAV (picornavirus). Fecal-oral transmission. Incubation 15-50 days. Common in regions with poor sanitation. No chronic carrier state. Fulminant in <1% but higher risk in adults >50 and pre-existing liver disease.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Acute liver failure -> EMERGENCY; Acute hepatitis -> SUPPORTIVE care (usually outpatient); Pregnant or elderly -> OBSERVE more closely

historyFeatures.note: Appears after prodromal phase; dark urine precedes jaundice
Fecal-oral: contaminated food/water, shellfish, travel to endemic area
Household or close contact with known case

laboratory.name: IgM anti-HAV antibody
laboratory.indication: diagnostic for acute/recent HAV infection; positive at symptom onset, remains positive 4-6 months

laboratory.name: Total anti-HAV IgG (serology)
laboratory.indication: past infection or vaccination; IgG-positive, IgM-negative indicates immunity

laboratory.name: ALT and AST
laboratory.indication: marked elevation (500-5000 IU/L); ALT > AST in typical acute hepatitis

laboratory.name: Total and direct bilirubin
laboratory.indication: elevated in icteric phase; mixed conjugated and unconjugated

laboratory.name: ALP and GGT
laboratory.indication: mildly elevated; if markedly elevated, consider cholestatic variant

laboratory.name: INR/PT
laboratory.indication: prolonged in severe disease; marker of synthetic dysfunction

laboratory.name: HAV RNA by PCR
laboratory.indication: confirmatory; used for outbreak investigation and research

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: hepatomegaly; gallbladder wall thickening; rule out obstruction

management.protocolReference: WHO Guidelines for Hepatitis A; CDC Recommendations for HAV; AASLD Practice Guidelines

pathways.criteria: IgM anti-HAV+, moderate ALT elevation, no coagulopathy, no encephalopathy
pathways.treatment: Supportive care: rest as needed, adequate nutrition, maintain hydration, avoid alcohol and hepatotoxic medications. Most recover fully within 4-8 weeks. No specific antiviral therapy. Monitor LFTs and INR weekly initially. Isolation: standard and contact precautions for 2 weeks after onset of jaundice (or 1 week after symptom onset). Case reporting to public health authorities.

pathways.criteria: Prolonged jaundice >8 weeks, bilirubin >10, INR >1.5, or cholestatic pattern
pathways.treatment: Hospital admission for monitoring. Supportive care. Monitor for acute liver failure. Cholestatic variant may benefit from ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 13-15mg/kg/day. Transplant evaluation if fulminant. Most still recover fully.

pathways.criteria: INR >1.5 + encephalopathy; <1% of cases; higher risk in adults >50 and pre-existing liver disease
pathways.treatment: ICU admission. Transplant center consultation. King's College Criteria for liver transplant. NAC IV may be considered (limited evidence). Supportive intensive care. Survival with transplant is excellent (>80%). Spontaneous recovery possible even with encephalopathy in young patients.

pathways.criteria: Unvaccinated person exposed to HAV within past 14 days
pathways.treatment: HAV vaccine single dose (if ≤40 years, healthy) OR immune globulin 0.02 mL/kg IM (if >40 years, immunocompromised, chronic liver disease, or <12 months). Give within 14 days of exposure. Household contacts, sexual contacts, and childcare contacts of confirmed case should receive PEP.

disease.emergencyPriority: medium if uncomplicated; high if fulminant
